section.title: Blessed Is He Who Considers the Poor
section.sectionRationale: The last psalm of Book I, David moves from a beatitude on caring for the poor, to his own prayer for healing under sickness and sin, to the sharpest betrayal in the psalm, a close friend who ate his bread turning against him, a verse Jesus applies directly to Judas. It closes with the doxology that ends Book I of the Psalter.
section.thesis: David's prayer for healing under sickness and sin is deepened by the betrayal of a trusted friend who shared his bread, a wound Jesus takes upon Himself when He identifies Judas's betrayal with this very verse, before the psalm closes in confident praise that opens onto the doxology ending Book I.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the opening beatitude on considering the poor, verses 1-3; David's own prayer for healing amid confessed sin, verses 4-9; the sharp betrayal by a close friend, verse 9's center; and the closing plea and doxology, verses 10-13.

units.label: Blessed is the one who cares for the poor

verses.text: Blessed is the one who cares for the poor; the LORD will deliver him in the day of trouble.
The LORD will protect and preserve him; He will bless him in the land and refuse to surrender him to the will of his foes.
The LORD will sustain him on his bed of illness and restore him from his bed of sickness.

units.label: Heal me, for I have sinned against You

verses.text: I said, "O LORD, be gracious to me; heal me, for I have sinned against You."
My enemies say with malice: "When will he die and be forgotten?"

My visitor speaks falsehood; he gathers slander in his heart; 
he goes out and spreads it abroad.
All who hate me whisper against me; they imagine the worst for me:
"A vile disease has been poured into him; he will never get up from where he lies!"

units.label: He who shared my bread has lifted his heel against me

verses.text:
Even my close friend whom I trusted, the one who shared my {{lechem|bread}}, 
has lifted up his heel against me.

units.label: By this I know that You delight in me

verses.text: But You, O LORD, be gracious to me and raise me up, that I may repay them.
By this I know that You delight in me, for my enemy does not triumph over me.
In my integrity You uphold me and set me in Your presence forever.

Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, from everlasting to everlasting. 
Amen and Amen.

lechem.gloss: Bread, the staple food of daily life and a symbol of shared table fellowship and covenant trust. Sharing bread implied friendship and loyalty, which makes the betrayal that follows, 'has lifted up his heel against me,' so sharply painful, and why Jesus quotes this exact verse of Judas at the Last Supper's table, John 13:18.

basic.context: The last psalm of Book I opens with a beatitude on the one who cares for the poor, then turns personal, David praying for healing while confessing his own sin, surrounded by enemies who wish for his death and a visitor who spreads slander. At its center falls the psalm's sharpest wound, betrayal by a close, trusted friend who shared his bread, before the psalm turns to confident praise and closes Book I with a doxology.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm should be read as a whole, its opening beatitude on mercy toward the poor is not separate from its plea for personal healing, both flow from the same conviction that the LORD is gracious to those in need, while its central betrayal verse gains its fullest weight only when read alongside Jesus's own use of it.
basic.originalLanguages: Verse 9's betrayal centers on lechem, shared bread, the ordinary sign of table fellowship and covenant loyalty in the ancient world, which is precisely why a friend turning against the one who shared that bread cuts so deeply, and why Jesus applies this verse directly to Judas at the table of the Last Supper.
basic.typology: David's betrayal by a trusted table companion who shared his bread anticipates Christ's betrayal by Judas Iscariot, one of the Twelve who shared His table and even His bread at the Last Supper, a connection Jesus Himself draws explicitly.
basic.christ: Jesus quotes this psalm's verse 9 directly and applies it to His own betrayal: 'He who is eating My bread has lifted up his heel against Me,' John 13:18, spoken of Judas at the Last Supper's own table. Where David's betrayal was a bitter but survivable wound, Christ's betrayal by one who shared His bread led to the cross, yet even there God's purposes of salvation were being accomplished, showing this psalm's pattern of suffering unjustly at a friend's hand fulfilled and transformed in the gospel.
basic.god: God is shown as the one who delivers, protects, and sustains those who show mercy to the poor and the sick, who hears honest confession of sin, and whose presence forever, not the absence of betrayal, is what finally upholds the one in integrity.
basic.crossReferences: John 13:18 quotes this exact verse of Judas Iscariot's betrayal of Jesus at the Last Supper. Matthew 25:34-40 develops the opening beatitude's concern for the poor into care shown to Christ Himself in the least of these. Psalm 22 and Psalm 55 share this psalm's theme of betrayal by trusted friends, deepening into Christ's own experience.
basic.oneStory: David's mercy for the poor, honest confession under sickness, and bitter betrayal by a trusted friend who shared his bread anticipate Christ, who showed perfect mercy to the poor, bore sin though sinless, and was betrayed by Judas at His own table, fulfilling this very psalm on the way to the cross and the salvation it accomplished.

context.literary: Closes Book I of the Psalter, Psalms 1-41, moving from a general beatitude to personal lament and betrayal before ending, like each of the Psalter's five books, with a closing doxology, verse 13, 'Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, from everlasting to everlasting. Amen and Amen.'
context.historical: Attributed to David, likely written during a season of serious illness compounded by betrayal from someone within his own inner circle, whose identity is not named but whose act of turning on a table companion was recognized as a severe breach of covenant loyalty.
context.canonical: Verse 9 is quoted directly by Jesus in John 13:18 regarding Judas Iscariot's betrayal, making this one of the clearest instances of a specific psalm verse applied by Christ Himself to His own experience of suffering.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bless those who show mercy to the poor and sick as those who will themselves receive the LORD's care, to model honest confession of sin amid suffering, and to give voice to the particular anguish of betrayal by a trusted friend, closing in confident praise of God's faithfulness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm's opening beatitude prescribes active mercy toward the poor as a pattern that invites God's own care; its account of David's illness, slander, and betrayal describes his particular experience, offered as a pattern for honest lament and confession under similarly compounded suffering.

debates.question: Who is the close friend of verse 9 who betrayed David, and does it matter for interpreting the psalm?

views.label: The friend's identity, likely Ahithophel during Absalom's rebellion or another trusted counselor, is historically significant and grounds the psalm in a specific, real betrayal within David's own reign
views.case: 2 Samuel 15-17 records Ahithophel, David's trusted counselor, defecting to Absalom's rebellion, a betrayal matching this psalm's description of a close, trusted companion turning against the king.

views.label: The friend's specific identity matters less than the pattern of betrayal itself, since Jesus applies the verse typologically to Judas without needing David's original friend to be historically identical to Judas in every particular
views.case: John 13:18 quotes the verse simply as fulfilled in Judas without requiring or asserting a one-to-one historical identity between David's betrayer and Judas, treating the pattern of trusted betrayal as what carries forward.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms that this psalm gives voice to the particular anguish of betrayal by someone deeply trusted, a pattern true to David's own experience and taken up by Jesus to describe His own far weightier betrayal by Judas.

study.originalLanguages: The betrayal in verse 9 turns on lechem, shared bread, the ordinary token of table fellowship and covenant loyalty, which is exactly why Jesus quotes this verse of Judas at the table of the Last Supper, John 13:18, where shared bread should have meant shared loyalty.

typology.type: The trusted friend who shared David's bread yet betrayed him, verse 9
typology.fulfillment: Judas Iscariot, who shared Christ's bread at the Last Supper yet betrayed Him, John 13:18.

typology.type: The beatitude on considering the poor, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: Christ's identification of Himself with the poor and needy, Matthew 25:34-40, and His own perfect mercy toward the poor throughout His ministry.

study.christ: Jesus applies this psalm's verse 9 directly to His own betrayal: 'He who is eating My bread has lifted up his heel against Me,' John 13:18, spoken of Judas Iscariot at the very table of the Last Supper. David's bitter wound of betrayal by a trusted table companion becomes, in Christ, the pattern of the ultimate betrayal, one of His own Twelve turning against Him, yet through that very betrayal God accomplished the salvation this whole psalm's confidence in the LORD's deliverance anticipates.
study.god: God is revealed as the one who delivers and sustains those who show mercy to the poor and sick, who hears honest confession even amid deserved discipline, and whose upholding presence, not the absence of betrayal, is what finally proves His delight in the one who trusts Him.

crossReferences.note: Jesus quotes this exact verse of Judas Iscariot's betrayal at the Last Supper's table, fulfilling this psalm directly.
Develops the opening beatitude's concern for the poor into care shown to Christ Himself in the least of these.
Shares this psalm's anguish over betrayal by a close, trusted companion, deepening the pattern Christ Himself experienced.

study.oneStory: David's mercy toward the poor, honest confession under sickness, and anguish at betrayal by a trusted friend who shared his bread anticipate Christ, who showed perfect mercy to the poor and was Himself betrayed by Judas at His own table, this very psalm's words fulfilled on the way to the cross and the salvation it secured.

quiz.q: What does the opening beatitude in verses 1-3 promise to the one who cares for the poor?

options.text: The LORD will deliver, protect, preserve, bless, and sustain him, even on his bed of illness.
options.note: This chain of promises links active mercy toward the poor with the LORD's own care in return.

options.text: He will become wealthy immediately as a direct reward.
options.note: The promises concern deliverance, protection, and sustaining care, not an immediate promise of wealth.

options.text: He will never face any trouble at all.
options.note: The text promises deliverance 'in the day of trouble,' implying trouble may come but God will act within it, not that it will never occur.

options.text: He will be exempt from ever needing to confess sin.
options.note: The beatitude concerns caring for the poor; David himself, presumably one who has shown such mercy, still confesses sin in verse 4.

quiz.q: What does David confess about himself in verse 4?

options.text: He asks God to be gracious and heal him, confessing, 'I have sinned against You.'
options.note: David's prayer for healing is joined directly to honest personal confession of sin.

options.text: He claims complete innocence of any wrongdoing.
options.note: The text is an explicit confession of sin, not a claim of innocence.

options.text: He blames his enemies entirely for his illness.
options.note: Verse 4 is personal confession, 'I have sinned against You'; enemies are addressed separately in later verses.

options.text: He refuses to pray about his illness at all.
options.note: Verse 4 is itself a direct prayer for healing, not a refusal to pray.

quiz.q: What happens in verse 9, and how does Jesus use this verse?

options.text: A close, trusted friend who shared his bread betrays him; Jesus quotes this exact verse of Judas's betrayal at the Last Supper, John 13:18.
options.note: This direct New Testament quotation is one of the clearest instances of a psalm verse applied by Jesus to His own experience.

options.text: A stranger David had never met betrays him.
options.note: The verse specifically describes a close, trusted friend, not a stranger, which is what makes the betrayal so sharp.

options.text: Jesus never references this verse anywhere in the Gospels.
options.note: John 13:18 quotes this verse explicitly regarding Judas.

options.text: The betrayal is only symbolic, with no literal act described.
options.note: The text describes a literal act, 'has lifted up his heel against me,' a concrete image of hostile betrayal.

quiz.q: According to verse 11, how does David know that God delights in him?

options.text: Because his enemy does not triumph over him.
options.note: David locates evidence of God's delight in his enemy's failure to prevail, not in the absence of all hardship.

options.text: Because he has never experienced any sickness.
options.note: The psalm explicitly describes David's illness earlier; his confidence in verse 11 concerns his enemy's defeat, not freedom from sickness.

options.text: Because all his friends remained loyal to him.
options.note: Verse 9 describes a close friend's betrayal, so loyalty from all friends is not the basis given for God's delight.

options.text: Because he has become wealthy.
options.note: The stated basis is his enemy's defeat and his own uprightness, not wealth.

quiz.q: How does this psalm close, and what is its significance for the Psalter's structure?

options.text: With the doxology, 'Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, from everlasting to everlasting. Amen and Amen,' closing Book I of the Psalms.
options.note: Each of the Psalter's five books closes with a doxology, and this verse marks the end of Book I, Psalms 1-41.

options.text: With a curse against David's enemies.
options.note: The closing verse is a doxology of praise to God, not a curse against enemies.

options.text: With silence, the psalm simply ending mid-lament.
options.note: The psalm explicitly closes with a full doxology of praise, not an abrupt or silent ending.

options.text: With a promise that David would never face illness again.
options.note: The closing doxology is praise to God's eternal nature, not a specific promise about future illness.

questions: The psalm opens by blessing those who care for the poor. Who is someone poor, sick, or struggling in your life right now that you could actively consider and care for this week?

David prays for healing while honestly confessing, 'I have sinned against You.' Is there a place where you are seeking God's help while avoiding honest confession of what caused or compounds your struggle?

The betrayal by a close friend who shared David's bread is the psalm's sharpest wound. Have you experienced betrayal by someone you deeply trusted, and how does knowing Jesus experienced this exact pattern, quoting this very verse of Judas, meet you in that wound?

David says he knows God delights in him because his enemy does not triumph over him, not because all trouble disappeared. Where in your life might you look for evidence of God's delight in the wrong places?

The psalm closes Book I of the Psalter with unrestrained doxology, even after confessing sin and betrayal. What would it look like for your own prayer life to move toward praise even in the middle of unresolved pain?

translationNotes.note: Poor renders a Hebrew word for the weak, needy, or helpless, and 'considers' implies careful, active attention rather than mere passing awareness.

Bread renders lechem, the staple food symbolizing shared table fellowship and covenant loyalty; 'lifted up his heel' is an idiom for open, contemptuous hostility from someone previously trusted. Jesus quotes this verse directly of Judas in John 13:18.
This doxology closes Book I of the Psalter, Psalms 1-41; each of the Psalter's five books ends with a similar closing blessing.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 41:1-13. No em or en dashes present. No sermon from the trusted cluster could be confirmed on Psalm 41 this session, so sermon status is none.
